speaker: interviewer
text: OK, when we left off, you were talking about the two different governments.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said the one government actually tried to help.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- you want to go ahead and pick it up where you-- where you left off? You said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The-- like I said, the government supported the Jews, even financially and morally. They said, everything will be fine. All the murderers would be brought to justice and stuff. And the other government said, there'll be no room for Jews in Poland. And those were actively [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] trying to find Jews and to hand them over to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Collaborating. And your sister-in-law was one step ahead of-- I mean, she-- you said she moved around a lot of the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. She had it very, very hard. She did a tremendous job. She had to care for herself, care for us. And she cared more for-- for us than for herself. Because she was in very bad situations herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewer
text: So she [? cared. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: After the-- after the war, we stayed for awhile [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in a little I would say a resort. It used to be a resort. But it was all abandoned. There was nobody there. So we stayed there for awhile. And then we went to Lodz. Warsaw was completely destroyed. And I couldn't find my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- there was nothing to-- to hold us there. So-- and there was no place to live in Warsaw anyway. So we went to Lodz. And why Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because we didn't want to sit there on the border. Because the armies were on the-- on the river. So we didn't know whether the Germans would now counterattack, and try to take it back or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we want to Lodz. And we settled there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed there until '46. In-- in 1946, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] we went to Germany. OK. I want to go back before-- before we get to there. I want to find out from you-- you had mentioned at the end of the other tape, that your sister and brother-in-law came there. How were you able to keep in contact with them? I mean, there was no phone. How-- how did they know-- how did you all keep in contact with each other? Well, everything from Mala. She was the one who was outside. And she could-- she could talk to people. They-- they used to meet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was wearing a person who was responsible of helping the people who needed help. There were a lot of people risking their lives to help others. Non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jews too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because she was risking her life,

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mrs. Mochska. She was risking her life. Because if-- if the Gestapo, the SS, would find out that she kept Jews-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at least, in a best scenario, she would be in jail. But you could be killed the same way as we would.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the SS or the Germans ever stop at the villa where you are living and-- and search or look around? Was she ever confronted?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only time they came was at-- when they-- when they-- when they were evacuating this area. They came in the morning. They were taking off the logistics, the wiring and stuff. And they brought some Ersatz coffee. And they said they'll be back at 2:00 the same day. And they would like to have some coffee ready. We mingled with them. Well, they were soldiers. We mingled with them. They were in the apartment, going in and out. And we didn't bother hiding anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we-- you know, we thought that they are so down that they-- there is a distinction between the-- Wehrmacht, which is the soldiers, the military part, and the-- and the SS. Those were ordinary people. You know, they are conscripted into the army. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you weren't afraid of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at-- at this stage of the game. This was a few days before. Because a day or two afterwards, their daughter came in, Mrs. Mochska's daughter, which I would say she was in her late 20s, early 30s. And she said to me-- in Polish, of course-- there are so many people on the road. So I went out, and I-- and I looked. And I saw the Russians are coming. [LAUGHS] And the first-- the first Russian soldier was a oriental, very short, very dirty, and very smelly. And I grabbed him, and I kissed him. And-- he had no belt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I kissed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to me, if I have vodka? This was the first question. I would have given him anything I had. But I didn't have any vodka. So you were liberated by the Russians, when they came in. How did the Russians treat the population when they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, afterwards, it was a different story. They set up their artillery not far from where we lived. And one evening, I was walking home. And I shortcutted through a field. And I got stopped by sentry. And he took me to this commandant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he started asking me questions, whether I have seen any-- any airplanes flying? Whether I have seen anything-- if I'm not a spy. I said to him, I am not a spy for the Germans. I don't know whether I'm living or I'm dead. I'm just-- I just stepped out from a bad dream. So he said to me, I don't believe you. You must be a spy for the Germans. And he kept me there until late at night. I pleaded with him. I said, there's a curfew. I can't walk after 9 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you're not walking anywheres. You're walking with us now. This-- this was a heartwarming story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the next day we moved. I didn't want to stay with them either. So we moved to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Plus she had told you it's time to leave. How is it that when you were living in hiding, did you go outside? Or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only at right. We used to go into the garden. Once in awhile, we would pick a plum, which was that size--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you lived on?

speaker: interviewee
text: --out of the tree. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- it was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there places that you-- you mentioned the one time when the woman saw you, when you were standing against the wall. But were there places that you would hide, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I built a false wall and put some shelves over it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: It worked good. It work when they came. When the Germans came into the house, we all went in there. But afterwards-- you see, they tried to take the population, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] male population, at least, with them. It was young men. They might use them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So some member from her family came to hide here with her. He thought that, well, she's a widow alone. So we stayed together. He didn't ask any questions. And I didn't-- I didn't volunteer anything. So he just thought you were part of her family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: He never asked you who you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, everybody was on the edge. Everybody-- nobody knew what's happening, whether the Germans are moving or staying longer or whatever, or whether they're gonna attack again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to just go back briefly. When-- when you-- when you were in the ghetto, and you had touched upon when your mother and your were taken-- your younger sister were taken-- why isn't it that they were with you? I mean, were they just-- you said that you were working on the railroad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they in-- ever in hiding? Or they just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out what happened? How they were picked up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody has-- had to work for the Germans, of course. My-- my father worked. And my father and my sister were in one shop, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] repairing uniforms. You know, they-- they took the uniforms from dead soldiers or-- I don't know. But anyway, those were uniforms in need of prepare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So they did it there. And my father-- and my mother and my sister were in another one, in another factory. They made rucksacks. Those are the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Backpacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Backpacks, right. And-- so what they did is they-- they would come and surround the whole factory, and tell everybody, all the workers, to go outside. And then they picked a number. Let's say if a factory employed 5,000 people, they would decide today, we'll take 1,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody was scrambling to-- not to be among those-- those 1,000. So the director of the factory had a lot to say. Because the Germans don't-- didn't care whether he takes A or B or C, as long as they have their quota. So when they-- when they took my mother and my sister, they were in need right away. Because they had empty space. Like I said, the-- the sideline-- the sidetrack was empty. And the train was coming-- was due in, an empty train. And they want to load it and keep it going. Was the-- this was a-- Round the clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: Constant, yeah, a constant operation. So they took my mother and my sister then. And my father couldn't even do a thing about it. He tried. He was-- he was devastated. And a few days later, he was caught himself. This was a story again, when they-- when they surrounded my father's-- the factory where my father worked. And they didn't know about it. And they ran to the fourth floor. They were hiding there. And the director of the factory called his name. And he was there. He was afraid to show his face. But finally, a-- a SS man went up there and told him to go down. And that's how they were saved. Because the-- the director of the factory told them, get in this line.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that save him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he didn't-- he didn't go. They demanded, I don't know what. They demanded a quota. And the directory of the factory put them in the group which has to stay. That's how he was saved. But he was-- he was picked up a few days later. And again, he jumped out of the train-- of the moving train-- he threw the coat. There were two little windows, in-- like in most freight cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- he-- he took off his jacket, his coat. And he told somebody there, throw out this jacket when I-- when I jump from this side. Because there were soldiers on top of the train, of the wagons. And they were shooting. So they were shooting it. When they saw this-- this coat flying out of the thing, they shot at the coat. In the meantime, he jumped from the other side. You understand what I'm saying? Mhm. A diversion.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how he saved himself. And he came back with a group of workers who were coming back to the ghetto like I did every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did--?

speaker: interviewee
text: He joined a group, and he walked in. They-- they let everybody in. You want to come in? Go ahead. So that's how. My story was we worked at the railroad until March of 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When they-- oh. They had those selections. When there was about 50,000 people left out a half a million--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they assembled everybody on a big, big plaza. And every group separately. And they were supposed to go to a selection. And there-- by the whim of the SS men, they would say, right or left. This was your death destiny. If you go to the left, you die. If you go to the right, you live. And out of this group-- we had over 250 people working-- they let 136. The rest was taken away. And they put us up in those freight cars. We were living there. And I've seen the writing on the wall. Because I thought someday they'll come, surround this whole thing, and take everybody away. So we decided to run. And I left the group one night and didn't come back. And of course, they took the-- a few days later. They took everybody away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you run to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had this-- this place, which my sister, with Mala again, has rented, with a friend. So I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you being guarded? How did you escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This-- it-- it wasn't guarded. We were on the-- on the tracks. The train goes on the tracks. And I just left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody else wanted to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I didn't announce that I'm gonna leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you would do something like that, you just kind of on your own--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I just left and I went to this apartment. I knew where their apartment is. And I was let in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sure enough, they were taken away. Now, when did you marry your wife, your first wife, Bala?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her in-- she comes from Kalisz, which is on the German border, not far from the German/Polish border. And her father was in the same line of business as my father. And one day, I went to see her father on an errand from my father. And that's how we met. And there was a lot going on. There was a lot of-- world-class symphony orchestra in Warsaw. There were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] plays. There was four or five theatres, which, by the order of the Germans, had to have only-- play only plays by Jewish writers. You couldn't use any-- any non-Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a lot of culture going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to the apartment, my wife was there already. And my father-in-law, being a religious man, said, you have to get married. Even in this apartment, in those conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got married. And we had a very elaborate wedding, 19 people. And a festive meal. She bought a couple of cupcakes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was everything. But we had a very good life together. [CRYING] [DRINKS] So you were married when you were in the apartment, with the 19 of you? And that was your wedding party?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- were you able to keep in contact? Or you don't know what happened to the other people, the 19 other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. But I think they all survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were all from different areas? It wasn't that you knew each other from before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- what was the closest you ever came to being discovered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this time when the one who came back to buy the flowers. And then was the incident with her divorced husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you closed all the windows and locked him out of the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. This was the-- these were the only two cases. Yeah, we were pretty lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were. So that-- you were just always one step ahead? Or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. It's-- it's funny. After this situation, where the-- the Russians came in, the next day when we started to moving out, because she thought this is proper, we were walking the street, the Main Street. And there were tens of thousands of people going in both directions. Because some people thought, oh, if you move north it will be better. And some thought that if we move south it would be better. But anyway, there was a lot, a lot of people on the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was walking the street there, too, with all the others. And a man stops in front of me and says to me, where did your hide? I wish I would know about you yesterday. This put a chill down my spine. He meant it. He meant business. Who was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was somebody who didn't like me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish citizen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a-- just a ordinary man who didn't like Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he says, if he would only found me-- only find me yesterday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He would probably denounce me. He would probably bring me to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean, what did you do when he said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just looked at him. I didn't say a word.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Russians came, and-- as all this was going on, did you-- when you went back to Warsaw, what was-- what did you see when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just looking for my father. I thought I'll get some information.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the house was destroyed. People used to live-- write something on the walls, on the remaining walls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, I am-- they put their name down. And they put their address where they're gonna be. But I didn't find anything about my father, no message, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people would leave messages for each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on the walls. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- when-- what was your feeling when you first came to town? And-- how did you feel when you saw  that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was a chaos, all over. We didn't know where to go. It's getting-- it was getting dark. And we had to find a place to sleep. So this again was-- we hooked up with another couple. And they said, they have-- they know a place. They know a-- they know an address where they can go. There's a-- a house still intact, where you can at least have a roof over your head. So we went there. And we slept over the night-- stayed overnight, the night. And the next day, we moved on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Warsaw? Just the one day?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day. And that's when you decided to go to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did your wife have family there? Was she looking for people there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mala came from-- lived in Lodz after-- after she got married. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they couldn't get their old apartment. It was occupied by somebody else. So they had to go rent another apartment. We used to come, but we had our own apartment. But we used to meet, almost daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said you rented an apartment, what-- what were you doing for work? What were you doing for-- for money?

speaker: interviewee
text: At-- at the beginning, the government helped people displaced and people without apartments, without places to live. Because, you see, the Germans took over all the nice apartment. And they requested furniture and-- and stuff. So there were a lot of empty apartments too, which they have left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we got support from the-- from the-- HIAS.

speaker: interviewer
text: HIAS. Now, what-- did you-- where did you want to go? Did you want to stay? Did you want to go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want-- you mentioned an uncle in Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got in touch with my uncle. And the situation in Palestine was very bad then. It was hunger and no housing. So he wanted to see us very, very much. But he advised us not to come, wait a little longer. The situation will improve, and then we will come. And in the meantime, we had an alternative either to go to Israel, stay in Germany, go to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After we got this rejection-- I should call it-- from my uncle, we decided to go to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you have to wait to get in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was three years. You were on the waiting list. You were in the quarters. And one day, they let you know that if you want to go, you have two weeks or three weeks or four weeks to prepare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was yours a large-- before the war, was yours a large family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a very large family. But a very close one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the war, how many were left?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] My sister in Israel, and the uncle in-- in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And a couple of cousins. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, your sister did go to Israel, I mean-- right after she was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many brothers and sisters did your mother have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had two brothers and four sisters, four or five sisters. Four sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that your father, he had no siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father?

speaker: interviewer
text: Brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: None.

speaker: interviewer
text: So no one made it. When you-- where were you when you found out that the war was actually over? Do you remember that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it didn't impress us as much. Because we-- we knew it's coming. We saw they are pulling back and back, further and further. And we know it's coming. So we weren't so shocked by it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't a surprise?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the anti-Semitism was still pretty strong. How-- how did the Polish, you know, the rest of the citizens, how did they treat you when you were trying to get your life back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you one story. This will reflect the whole situation. There were-- there was-- there's a town in Poland called Kiecle, which is a big textile center. And people usually came back to their place of birth or where they lived before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a house which was owned by the Jewish community before the war. A few hundred people who came back from the-- those-- from this region, settled in this building. And one day, somebody-- they found a Christian boy killed, a young kid. And somebody spread the rumor that the Jews killed him because they-- they need blood for matzoh. The mob stormed this building. This was a year after the war-- after the war ended.

speaker: interviewee
text: They stormed this building. They pulled out 72 people and killed them with rocks, with picks, with knives. They killed 72 people. This was 50 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kiecle. So this was the attitude.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned something, and I-- it triggered a question that I wanted to ask you, which was when you were in-- you know what? Why don't we stop right here. 'Cause--s
